Chapter 6
Clinical pathology
Livia Benato and Elisabetta Mancinelli
Knowledge of the clinical pathology of small pet mammals  tigations to make a definitive diagnosis and plan 
has improved over the years and many research studies  appropriate treatment.
have been carried out to better understand how to aid  Haematological and biochemical evaluations are 
the diagnosis of medical conditions using laboratory  often required as part of early investigation. The inter-
tests. Many owners now expect and are prepared to  pretation of the results can sometimes be challenging 
undertake more advanced diagnostic testing for their pet  as it is often not known how common diseases in guinea 
guinea pigs and, although many aspects are still unknown,  pigs affect haematological and biochemical parameters. 
interpretation can be extrapolated from studies con- For this reason, results should be interpreted alongside 
ducted on laboratory animals or other pet rodents. clinical signs and findings from other investigations. Few 
Guinea pigs are prey species and tend to mask signs  published references of laboratory findings associated 
of disease. Once a clinical examination has been per- with different clinical presentations have been reported 
formed, it is often necessary to carry out further inves- (Figure 6.1).
Presentation  Laboratory findings References
Anorexia Haematology is usually normal but leucocytosis, anaemia  Mayer and Donnelly, 2013

arus caviae)

omma americanum and  Szabo et al., 2003

dehydration and azotaemia may be seen
